en.extract:
Cabanis's greenbul (Phyllastrephus cabanisi), also known as Cabanis's bulbul, is a species of songbird in the bulbul family, Pycnonotidae. It is found in east-central and south-central Africa. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical dry forest, subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest, subtropical or tropical moist montane forest, and subtropical or tropical moist shrubland.


Taxonomy
Cabanis's greenbul was formally described in 1880 by the German ornithologist Jean Cabanis under the binomial name Trichophorus flaveolus based on a specimen that had been collected in Angola. Unfortunately, the scientific name was pre-occupied by Trichophorus flaveolus that had been introduced by John Gould in 1836 for the white-throated bulbul, now Alophoixus flaveolus. In 1882 the English ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe proposed the replacement name, Criniger cabanisi, with the specific epithet chosen to honour Cabanis who had first described the species. Cabanis's greenbul is now one of 20 greenbuls placed in the genus Phyllastrephus that was introduced in 1832 by William Swainson. The genus name combines the Ancient Greek φυλλον/phullon  meaning "leaf" with στρεφω/strephō meaning "to toss" or "to turn".
Three subspecies are recognised:

P. c. cabanisi (Sharpe, 1882) – central Angola to southeast DR Congo, west Tanzania and north Zambia
P. c. sucosus Reichenow, 1904 – south Sudan and west Kenya to east DR Congo and northwest Tanzania
P. c. placidus (Shelley, 1899) – east Kenya through Tanzania to northeast Zambia, Malawi and northwest Mozambique
The subspecies P. c. placidus has sometimes been considered as a separate species, the placid greenbul.

'''Cabanis's greenbul''' ('''''Phyllastrephus cabanisi'''''), also known as '''Cabanis's bulbul''', is a species of [[songbird]] in the [[bulbul]] family, Pycnonotidae. It is found in east-central and south-central Africa. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical dry forest]], [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]], subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]], and [[subtropical or tropical moist shrubland]].

==Taxonomy==
Cabanis's greenbul was [[species description|formally described]] in 1880 by the German ornithologist [[Jean Cabanis]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Trichophorus flaveolus'' based on a specimen that had been collected in Angola.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Cabanis | first=Jean | author-link=Jean Cabanis | year=1880 | title=Neue Vögel aus Angola | language=German | journal=Ornithologisches Centralblatt | volume=5 | page=174 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8738784 }}</ref> Unfortunately, the scientific name was pre-occupied by ''Trichophorus flaveolus'' that had been introduced by John Gould in 1836 for the [[white-throated bulbul]], now ''Alophoixus flaveolus''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1836 | title=''Trichophorus flaveolus'' | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume=4 | issue=37 | pages=6-7 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30570731 }}</ref> In 1882 the English ornithologist [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] proposed the [[Nomen novum|replacement name]], ''Criniger cabanisi'', with the specific epithet chosen to honour Cabanis who had first described the species.<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Sharpe | first1=R. Bowdler | author-link=Richard Bowdler Sharpe | date=1881 | publication-date=1882 | title=Catalogue of the Passeriformes or Perching Birds in the Collection of the British Museum. Cichlomorphae: Part III. Containing the first portion of the family Timeliidae (Babbling-Thrushes) | series=Catalogue of the Birds in the British Museum | volume=6 | location=London | publisher=Trustees of the British Museum | page=83 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8305873 }} Although the title page bears the date  1881, this volume was not published until 1882.</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=269 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480944 }}</ref> Cabanis's greenbul is now one of 20 greenbuls placed in the genus ''[[Phyllastrephus]]'' that was introduced in 1832 by [[William Swainson]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Bulbuls | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/bulbuls/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=6 March 2025}}</ref> The genus name combines the [[Ancient Greek]] φυλλον/''phullon '' meaning "leaf" with στρεφω/''strephō'' meaning "to toss" or "to turn".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=Phyllastrephus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=Phyllastrephus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=15 March 2025 }}</ref>

Three [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''P. c. cabanisi'' ([[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe]], 1882) – central Angola to southeast DR Congo, west Tanzania and north Zambia
* ''P. c. sucosus'' [[Anton Reichenow|Reichenow]], 1904 – south Sudan and west Kenya to east DR Congo and northwest Tanzania
* ''P. c. placidus'' ([[George Ernest Shelley|Shelley]], 1899) – east Kenya through Tanzania to northeast Zambia, Malawi and northwest Mozambique

The subspecies ''P. c. placidus'' has sometimes been considered as a separate species, the placid greenbul.<ref name=ioc/>
